text.content:
You died. That was always going to happen. You are Unkindled — ash that the First Flame saw fit to raise again — and the world has gone cold and hollow around you. From the Cemetery of Ash you will climb through a dying kingdom to the gate of the Boreal Valley, where something vast and frostbound waits.

This is a Dark-Souls-flavoured one-shot. A few things are true here that aren't true in most D&D:

  • It is hard, and death is normal. You will probably die. Dying is a mechanic, not a failure — you'll get back up at the last bonfire and try again, a little wiser.
  • Defence is a skill, not a stat. Whether you dodge, block, or parry an attack — and when — is a choice you make every time you're hit. Reading the enemy is the whole game.
  • You build by choosing. Every level you pick raw power or magic, never both. The tradeoff is the character.

Don't worry about the rules below being long — you'll have them in hand, and your DM runs the fiddly bits. Pick your hero on the next page, skim the two after, and you're ready.

Ten ready-made heroes wait in the Cemetery of Ash. Each is a complete, levelled, pick-up-and-play character — weapons, stats, and cards already set. You don’t build anything and you never do the math; just take the one whose fight you want to fight. Read its sheet, swap before we start if it doesn’t fit, and you’re ready.

Start here: what do you want to do?

  • Stand in front and refuse to fall → Knight
  • Hit one thing very, very hard → Warrior
  • Never stop moving → Mercenary
  • Hold the line and keep the party standing → Herald
  • Stab things in the back → Thief
  • Vanish, then ruin someone’s day → Assassin
  • Delete enemies from across the room → Sorcerer
  • Set the whole room on fire → Pyromancer
  • Be the reason everyone survives → Cleric
  • Build your own thing from scratch → Deprived

The roster

Hero5e classKey statLeans towardWhat you’ll be doing
Knight (Tank)FighterSTRBlockSword & shield; soak hits and hold the line so the squishies live. The easiest seat at the table.
Warrior (Bruiser)BarbarianSTRSoak / dodgeTwo-handed greataxe; rage through the pain and hit like a falling tower. Simple, brutal, durable.
Mercenary (Skirmisher)FighterDEXDodge / parryTwin curved blades and constant motion — circle a foe and punish every opening.
Herald (Battle-healer)PaladinSTR & FaithBlockSpear, shield, and prayer: fight in the front rank and keep the party on its feet.
Thief (Striker)RogueDEXDodgeDaggers and footwork; slip behind a foe and Sneak Attack for a huge spike. Hits hard, folds fast.
Assassin (Trickster)RogueDEXDodge / parryAn estoc, stealth, and a whisper of sorcery — disappear, then strike from nowhere.
Sorcerer (Arcane DPS)WizardINTDodgeSoul magic that erases enemies at range. A glass cannon: lethal kept safe, doomed caught close.
Pyromancer (Blaster)SorcererCHADodgeFire by the fistful, great against crowds — and Andre can stoke your flame stronger.
Cleric (Healer)ClericWISBlockMiracles that mend the party and smite the hollow. The lifeline everyone leans on.
Deprived (Wildcard)FighterYour callAnyNaked but for a club — and three free attribute points to build literally anything.

Two questions that decide everything

1. Steel or sorcery? Martials (Knight, Warrior, Mercenary, Herald, Thief, Deprived) hit reliably, take a beating, and never run dry. Casters (Sorcerer, Pyromancer, Cleric — with the Assassin and Herald dabbling) trade fragility for power that runs on charges and refills at the bonfire. A five-person party is happiest with a frontline tank, one or two strikers, a healer, and a caster — but honestly, any mix works.

2. How much do you want to gamble on defence? Every hero can dodge, block, or parry each incoming hit — it’s a skill, not a stat — but the kits lean. Shielded heroes (Knight, Herald, Cleric) block safely for low risk; nimble ones (Mercenary, Thief, Assassin) dodge and parry for big payoffs; fragile casters mostly dodge and keep their distance.

The Deprived — for tinkerers only

The Deprived starts with nothing but a club and three unspent attribute points, and you may swap its cards freely. It’s the build-your-own seat: strong in the right hands, but the only one that asks you to make choices up front. New to the table? Take a named hero. Love fiddling with builds? This is your chair.

First time playing anything like this? The most forgiving picks are the Knight (block and survive), the Warrior (swing and smash), or the Cleric (heal and matter). All three are simple and always useful.

You can’t choose wrong — every sheet is already balanced, and your DM handles the fiddly bits. Pick the fight you want to fight; we’ll teach you the rest at the table. Praise the bonfire.

pages.name: How Combat Works (the heart of it)

text.content:
On your turn you act normally (see the next page). The special part is what happens when an enemy attacks YOU.

Every incoming attack, you choose a reaction

Each enemy move plays out over three windows — W1 wind-up, W2 commit, W3 impact. As the DM narrates the swing, you call how you answer and when:

  • Dodge (free, unlimited) — roll a flat d20 vs the move's Dodge DC. The window you pick sets the quality: the right window rolls with advantage, a poor one with disadvantage, otherwise a normal roll. Success = take no damage and slip 5 ft (end up behind the foe → advantage on your next attack).
  • Block (costs your reaction, no roll) — a good shield knocks a set percentage off each damage type (more for a heavy shield, less for a buckler); it chips at low tiers and never staggers anyone. Charges, grabs and shield-kicks are unblockable — sidestep them instead. Reliable, low-risk, low-reward.
  • Parry (costs your reaction) — high risk, high reward. Roll a flat d20 vs the move's Parry DC: only the perfect window gives advantage; every other time you roll with disadvantage. Succeed and you negate the hit and stagger the enemy (a free riposte opening). Fail and you take the hit in full.

The catch

Each move has its own perfect windows, and some attacks are un-dodgeable, un-blockable, or un-parryable. You learn a boss by fighting it — watch the tells, remember what worked, share notes with the party. That's the puzzle. None of it keys off your stats: it's all get gud.

Strike

Your Attack action makes a weapon strike (two at level 5+). Roll to hit as normal (d20 + proficiency + your governing stat + any weapon upgrade). On a hit, damage is:

weapon dice  +  scaling bonus  +  upgrade (+N)

Your scaling bonus is one number you read straight off your weapon card (it lists the value for your stat). Heavily-scaling weapons reward pouring points into their stat; you never do mental math. See the Weapon Scaling & Upgrades sheet for the full grid.

Armor & damage reduction

Heavy armor (Knight's Plate, Chainmail) reduces incoming damage by 2-3 points per hit (minimum 1 damage always gets through). Light armor reduces by 1 point. This stacks with dodges, blocks, and parries — armor is your passive defense, reactions are your active reads. Wearing nothing? No reduction, but you move faster.

Move & position

Positioning matters: dodging behind a foe sets up advantage, and ranged enemies want you to close. Use cover — especially anywhere a dragon can see you.

Weapon Arts (if you have one)

Special techniques chosen at higher levels (Stomp, Quickstep, Perseverance, and the like). Each card explains itself; most cost an action or a reaction.

Casting (if you're a caster)

Spells run on charges, not 5e slots. Each spell lists a charge cost (Tier 1/2/3 = 1/2/3). Spend from your Spell Charges pool; it refills when you rest at a bonfire. Your catalyst also scales — add your Int or Faith value (from the catalyst card) to a spell's damage or healing.

Leveling (only at the bonfire)

At the Firelink bonfire the Fire Keeper turns banked souls into strength. It takes about a minute: pay the cost, bump your class level by 1 (the sheet auto-adds HP and, at level 5, a second attack), then make your one pick — +1 attribute OR a new spell. Fighters stack attributes and get tanky; casters take spells and stay fragile. Pick your lane. (Full menu: the Level-Up Menu Cards.)

Estus

Your Estus Flask heals 2d4+2 and starts with 3 charges. It refills every time you rest at a bonfire. Estus Shards (found or bought) add charges. Quaffing is an action — choosing when to drink mid-fight is its own gamble.

Souls

Souls are XP and currency. They come in two states: carried (on your body, at risk) and banked (spent-able, safe). Bank at the bonfire before you push forward.

Death & the bloodstain

When you fall, you respawn at the last bonfire — but your carried souls drop as a bloodstain where you died. Reach it again to reclaim them; die before you do and they're gone for good. Enemies return when you rest. Death costs you ground and souls, never the run. Get up. Try again. Praise the bonfire.

Cinders — the party’s lives

The party shares a pool of Cinders: the sparks that let you rise after a fall. Drop to 0 HP and the party spends one Cinder to haul you back up; wipe and one Cinder respawns you at the bonfire. They don’t come back when you rest — Cinders only run down. While they last, death is just a setback. When they run out, it stops being one. Don’t go Hollow.
